one_line_takeaway: PDLRecover recovers a poisoned or client-depleted fully-decentralized (server-less) model to near-retrain accuracy (e.g. 93.2%/93.4% vs 94.9%/94.6% retrain on FashionMNIST/HAR) while cutting recovery time by roughly 33-35% versus full retraining, by running Shamir-secret-shared L-BFGS Hessian-vector-product approximations across clients so no individual client's historical gradient is ever exposed.
key_finding: On three datasets (MNIST, FashionMNIST, HAR) with a backdoor/poison attack from 10-60 malicious clients (out of n=200, with n=3k-1), PDLRecover matches Drop-Client-Retrain accuracy (Drop Client Retrain ~93% on MNIST/FashionMNIST, ~94% on HAR; PDLRecover ~91% on MNIST/FashionMNIST, ~92% on HAR) while cutting running time: Table II shows PDLRecover saves 33% average running time versus Drop Client Retrain under attack (e.g. MNIST at 60 malicious clients: Retrain 607s vs PDLRecover 417s; FashionMNIST: Retrain 710s vs PDLRecover 465s; HAR: Retrain 1250s vs PDLRecover 901s), and Table III shows a 35% average running-time saving in the pure client-dropout (no attack) scenario. In the method-comparison table (Table IV), PDLRecover reaches 93.9% (MNIST), 93.2% (FashionMNIST), 93.4% (HAR) accuracy versus a full Retrain baseline of 95.4%/94.9%/94.6%, and versus three prior unlearning baselines cited as [14], [40], and [35]: on FashionMNIST those baselines get 86.5%/87.4%/85.8% (PDLRecover's 93.2% is markedly higher); on HAR they get 84.4%/86.5%/87.2% (PDLRecover's 93.4% is markedly higher); on MNIST they get 95.2%/95.3% (comparable to, slightly above, PDLRecover's 93.9%). Model-before-recovery (poisoned, uncorrected) accuracy is reported around 50%, and under maximal malicious-client counts, unmitigated training accuracy drops to ~24% (MNIST/FashionMNIST) and ~13% (HAR). The paper's own historical-information-only baseline (no secret sharing, direct reconstruction) degrades accuracy to ~75% under client dropout, motivating the SS-L-BFGS/periodic-refresh design over naive historical replay.
core_contribution: PDLRecover is a method for recovering a fully decentralized (peer-to-peer, server-less) learning model after it has been degraded by poisoned or dropped-out clients, without full retraining and without exposing any client's individual historical model updates. It extends the L-BFGS Hessian-vector-product (HVP) approximation technique (originally used for federated-server model recovery in prior work, e.g. Cao et al. and Jiang et al.) into a decentralized, privacy-preserving setting by (1) having clients secret-share their local gradients via Shamir's secret sharing rather than exposing them to a central server, (2) proving that Lagrange interpolation of secret shares commutes with summation so the SS-L-BFGS Hessian-vector product can be computed correctly over secret-shared quantities, and (3) structuring recovery into preparation rounds, periodic exact-update rounds (buffer refresh), quasi-Newton HVP-approximated rounds, and a final block of exact rounds to bound approximation error and restore accuracy without full retraining.
threat_model: Attacker controls a subset of malicious clients (fabricated identities or compromised legitimate clients) who can submit arbitrary model updates to degrade the global model via untargeted poisoning (raise overall test error) or targeted/backdoor poisoning (misclassify specific triggered inputs, e.g. red-stripe trigger on MNIST/FashionMNIST mapped to 'bird', or a zeroed 20th feature on HAR mapped to a target label). Two attacker knowledge levels are considered: partial-knowledge (attacker knows only its own clients' data/updates and the global model/loss) and full-knowledge (attacker knows all clients' data/updates and the aggregation rule). Separately, the paper's own security analysis (Section VII-B) defines a privacy threat model with external attackers (unauthorized parties who steal a client's stored secret-share buffer) and internal attackers (authorized-but-malicious clients who try to reconstruct another honest client's individual update from received subsecrets). The paper proves both attacker types fail unless they collude with more than the polynomial degree/threshold number of clients (denoted k) to recover any individual client's update.
limitations: The paper does not report communication overhead in absolute terms (only asymptotic O(.) cost expressions), nor does it quantify the cryptographic/quantization overhead of mapping real-valued gradients into finite-field fixed-point representation for Shamir secret sharing. No adversarial evaluation of colluding-client attacks against the secret-sharing scheme itself is empirically tested (only proved theoretically). The reviewed portion does not include an explicit theorem statement bounding ||recovered - retrained|| (Assumptions 1-2 are given but the resulting bound theorem's numeric form is beyond the extracted 22-page window). Evaluation is limited to three small/medium image and sensor classification datasets with 200 simulated clients and a synchronous fully-connected decentralized topology; no experiments on partial connectivity, asynchronous rounds, or larger/more realistic decentralized network topologies are reported in the reviewed sections. Malicious-client counts are capped at 60 of 200 (30%) per the n=3k-1 constraint tied to the secret-sharing threshold, so higher corruption fractions or adaptive/full-knowledge attackers are not empirically stress-tested.

datasets_used: MNIST (70,000 images, 60k train/10k test, ResNet50, 200 clients)
HAR - Human Activity Recognition (10,299 samples from 30 volunteers, 80/20 train/test split, 6 activity classes)
quantitative_results: Table II running time under attack: PDLRecover saves 33% average running time vs Drop Client Retrain (e.g. MNIST 60 malicious clients: 417s vs 607s; FashionMNIST: 465s vs 710s; HAR: 901s vs 1250s)
Table III running time under client dropout only: PDLRecover saves 35% average running time vs Drop Client Retrain

Table IV accuracy comparison: PDLRecover 93.9% (MNIST), 93.2% (FashionMNIST), 93.4% (HAR) vs Retrain 95.4%/94.9%/94.6% vs cited baselines [14] 95.2%/86.5%/84.4%, [40] 95.3%/87.4%/86.5%, [35] 94.7%/85.8%/87.2%
Unmitigated accuracy at maximum malicious clients: ~24% (MNIST/FashionMNIST), ~13% (HAR)
Historical-information-only baseline under client dropout: accuracy degrades to ~75%
Malicious client counts tested: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 (with n=200 total clients, n=3k-1)
baselines_compared: Drop Client Retrain (remove malicious clients, retrain from scratch)
Historical Information method (remove malicious clients, reconstruct directly from cached history without secret sharing/periodic refresh)
Cited prior unlearning/recovery methods labeled [14], [40], [35] in Table IV (federated-learning-oriented recovery/unlearning baselines from related work, not the paper's own contribution)
